CLARITY.justification: The paper is well organized and follows a clear structure (intro → method in Sec.2/3 → experiments in Sec.4 → conclusion) and the main contribution (the Gaussian attention model / TransGaussian) is stated early in the abstract and introduction. Notation is generally consistent and equations (1)–(7) make the core ideas readable, though a few parameter details (e.g. the small positive constant in Σ parameterization) and some symbol spacing are slightly unclear. Figures and tables are referenced appropriately (e.g., Fig.1–4, Tables 1–11) and examples (WorldCup2014 queries) are informative, but the provided text contains formatting glitches, duplicated lines, and a few minor grammatical/author-contact errors that hinder polish. Overall the exposition is clear and understandable with only minor presentation issues to fix.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper proposes a clear new application and extension of Gaussian embeddings: a Gaussian attention scoring function (Eq.1) and a TransGaussian KB model that represents relations with mean and covariance (Eq.2) enabling uncertainty propagation and compositional traversal via Gaussian convolution (Eq.4). While Gaussian embeddings (Vilnis & McCallum, 2014) and TransE/compositional training (Guu et al., 2015) are prior work, this paper non-trivially combines and extends those ideas to content-based neural memory addressing and QA (including conjunction via product of Gaussians), and explicitly contrasts its contributions with prior methods. The contribution is therefore notable and clearly differentiated, though building on existing techniques rather than introducing a wholly orthogonal paradigm.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper's main claims (Gaussian attention for controllable spread, compositional propagation by summing means/variances, and conjunction via product of Gaussians) are clearly stated and supported by derivations and targeted experiments on the WorldCup2014 and a WordNet subset. The core math (score as log Gaussian, path composition µ = v+∑δr and Σ = ∑Σr for discrete paths, and product-of-Gaussians giving additive quadratic terms in Eq.7) is correct; however the question-answering parametrization using continuous relation weights αr with Σe = ∑αr^2Σr is presented as a modeling choice without formal justification and is therefore heuristic. Experimental methodology is reasonable (filtered rank, H@1, TransE baselines, compositional training ablation) but limited: small, domain-specific data, oracle entity extraction, few baselines, and no statistical significance analysis, so generalization claims are not fully supported. Overall the work is technically sound but has some modeling heuristics and empirical limitations that warrant a cautious assessment.
